fuzzy_prd:
We need a server-side toolkit that connects a web framework to the output of an external front-end bundler. The bundler compiles JavaScript and stylesheets into a public directory and emits a manifest file — a JSON map from each logical asset name to the digest-fingerprinted URL it compiled to. Templates should reference stable logical names and have those resolved at runtime to the correct cache-busted URLs. The toolkit needs four things: first, resolve which named build environment is currently active from a configuration document and two ordered candidates (an explicit override and the host framework environment), falling back to a default when neither matches; second, derive all the project filesystem paths — source directory, entry subdirectory, output directory under public, manifest file path, build config directory — from a small configuration document relative to the project root, with defaults for any missing keys; third, look up a logical asset name in the compiled manifest and return the public URL and disk path, or fail cleanly with a category error when the name is absent; fourth, render view helpers that produce a public URL, a script tag, or a stylesheet link tag for a given asset name. Handle the manifest-not-found error the same way the auth module handles missing tokens — surface a category error with the offending name, not a stack trace.

One small follow-up from the team questions: the default path values should be source='app/javascript', entry='packs', output='packs', manifest='manifest.json', and config='config/webpack'. Also, the paths.yml location is not something we vary from config — it is always at 'config/webpack/paths.yml' under the root.

On environment selection, the fallback is always 'production'. The order is: first, if node_env override names a declared environment, use it; otherwise if rails_env names a declared environment, use it; otherwise fall back to 'production'. If the paths document is absent, treat the declared set as empty, which means resolution always returns 'production'.

Also, for the asset-missing case, the returned error output needs to be exactly 'manifest=<absolute_manifest_path>\nerror=asset_not_found\nname=<missing_name>\n'. The manifest line always comes first.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: module Webpacker::Helper; def javascript_pack_tag(name, **options); def stylesheet_pack_tag(name, **options); def asset_pack_path(name)

trigger_keywords: what HTML does the stylesheet helper emit
what link element is generated for a CSS asset
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: stylesheet_pack_tag emits: '<link rel="stylesheet" media="screen" href="/packs/<fingerprinted>.css" />\n'. javascript_pack_tag emits: '<script src="/packs/<fingerprinted>.js"></script>\n'. asset_pack_path returns just the URL path with a trailing newline.

trigger_keywords: where is the manifest file always located by default
what is the manifest path when using default config
how is the manifest path derived from root
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The manifest path is always: <root>/public/<output>/manifest.json. With defaults: <root>/public/packs/manifest.json. The output directory is: <root>/public/<output>, and the public directory is always <root>/public.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: Handle the manifest-not-found error the same way the auth module handles missing tokens — surface a category error with the offending name, not a stack trace
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Webpacker::Manifest.lookup raises Webpacker::FileLoader::NotFoundError when an asset is absent. The dispatcher catches this and emits 'manifest=...\nerror=asset_not_found\nname=<name>' — a neutral three-line response.

oracle_data.fallback_response: Please refer to the start.md specification. Actions are: resolve_env, config_paths, manifest, helper (with helper sub-field of asset_pack_path, javascript_pack_tag, or stylesheet_pack_tag).

feature1_resolve_env.json.description: Resolve which named build environment is active. The system reads the set of declared environment names from the path-configuration document, then resolves the active environment from two ordered candidates: an explicit build-tool override (NODE_ENV-style) and the host framework environment. The rule is: if the override names a declared environment, use it; otherwise if the framework environment names a declared environment, use it; otherwise fall back to the default environment named 'production'. The resolved name is reported together with a predicate stating whether it equals 'production'.

feature2_path_derivation.json.description: Derive the set of project filesystem paths from a path-configuration document, relative to the application root. The configuration supplies logical names for the JavaScript source directory, the entry subdirectory, the compiled-output subdirectory (under the application's public directory), the manifest filename, and the build-config directory. The system resolves: the raw source name; the absolute source directory; the entry directory (source directory joined with the entry name); the output directory (public directory joined with the output name); the manifest path (output directory joined with the manifest filename); the build-config directory; and the fixed location of the path-configuration document itself (always config/webpack/paths.yml under the root). Any key missing from the supplied configuration falls back to a built-in default.

cases.expected_output:
source=app/javascript
source_path=/app/app/javascript
entry_path=/app/app/javascript/packs
output_path=/app/public/packs
manifest_path=/app/public/packs/manifest.json
config_path=/app/config/webpack
file_path=/app/config/webpack/paths.yml

feature3_1_manifest_lookup.json.description: Look up a logical asset name in the compiled manifest and report its resolved references. The manifest is a map from a logical asset name (such as a JavaScript or stylesheet filename) to the digest-fingerprinted public URL produced by the build. For a name that exists in the manifest, the system reports the absolute location of the manifest file, the resolved public URL for the asset, and the absolute on-disk path under the public directory that the URL corresponds to.

feature3_2_manifest_missing.json.description: Look up a logical asset name that is absent from the compiled manifest. When the name is not present, the lookup fails rather than returning a value: the system reports the absolute location of the manifest file, a neutral error category indicating the asset could not be found, and the offending name.

feature4_1_asset_path.json.description: Compute the public asset URL for a named pack, for use in a view. Given the logical asset name, the helper looks the name up in the compiled manifest and returns the resolved digest-fingerprinted public URL exactly as stored in the manifest (an absolute URL path beginning with a slash).

feature4_2_javascript_tag.json.description: Render a script element for a named JavaScript pack. Given the logical asset name, the helper resolves the manifest-mapped public URL and emits a complete HTML script tag whose src attribute is that resolved URL. The output is the full self-contained tag.

feature4_3_stylesheet_tag.json.description: Render a stylesheet link element for a named stylesheet pack. Given the logical asset name, the helper resolves the manifest-mapped public URL and emits a complete HTML link tag for a screen-media stylesheet whose href attribute is that resolved URL. The output is the full self-contained tag.
